body:
{
  "title": "Crestline Capital",
  "compiled_truth": "Crestline Capital is a Series A–focused venture firm investing primarily in B2B SaaS and infrastructure companies. Founded in 2021, the firm has built a portfolio of roughly 60 companies, most of them in the enterprise middleware, developer tools, and data infrastructure categories. Crestline tends to lead rounds in the $8M–$18M range, occasionally co-leading with larger multi-stage funds when the round sizes creep upward.

The firm operates with a lean partnership structure. [Bex Andersen](people/bex-andersen) is the managing partner and the person most often seen at the table during early diligence. Bex has a reputation for moving fast — term sheets within two weeks of first meeting if the conviction is there — and for being unusually hands-on post-investment, particularly around go-to-market strategy. [Cal Suzuki](people/cal-suzuki) joined the partnership in late 2025 after spending several years on the operating side at an infrastructure startup; Cal's focus areas are data systems, security primitives, and anything that sits close to the wire. The two complement each other well: Bex brings the commercial lens, Cal brings the technical depth.

Crestline first appeared on Anchor Codec's radar in mid-2027 when Cal reached out after seeing a demo at a data infrastructure meetup in Portland. The firm was already tracking the deterministic data infrastructure space and had been looking for a company that could unify compression, authentication, and transmission in a way that didn't require multiple vendor contracts. Internal notes suggest Crestline's diligence process was thorough but not adversarial — they asked pointed questions about single-pass primitive performance under adversarial network conditions, and they wanted to understand the competitive moat beyond patents.

Crestline's broader portfolio includes several companies that could be relevant as design partners or integration targets for Anchor Codec, particularly in the observability and edge compute categories. The firm publishes a semi-annual "Infrastructure Conviction" memo that circulates among LPs and portfolio founders; the Spring 2028 edition reportedly included a section on deterministic pipelines as an emergent category.

One thing worth noting: Crestline has a strict no-board-seat policy for initial investments under $12M. They prefer an observer seat and informal monthly check-ins instread.",
  "timeline": "- **2026-03-15** | Crestline Capital closes Fund III at $340M, with expanded mandate for infrastructure investments.
- **2026-08-22** | [Cal Suzuki](people/cal-suzuki) publishes a blog post titled "Why Deterministic Beats Probabilistic in Data Pipelines" — gets moderate traction on developer forums.
- **2027-01-10** | Crestline leads a $14M Series A for an edge compute startup in their portfolio, signaling deeper infrastructure conviction.
- **2027-06-03** | [Cal Suzuki](people/cal-suzuki) attends a Portland data infrastructure meetup and sees an early Anchor Codec demo. Reaches out via email the following week.
- **2027-07-18** | First formal diligence call between Crestline and Anchor Codec; [Bex Andersen](people/bex-andersen) joins for the second half.
- **2027-09-02** | Crestline requests a technical deep-dive session focused on single-pass primitive throughput benchmarks.
- **2027-11-14** | Internal Crestline investment committee meets to discuss Anchor Codec; outcome unclear but follow-up meetings are scheduled.
- **2028-01-22** | Crestline's semi-annual "Infrastructure Conviction" memo references deterministic data infrastructure as an emergent category.
- **2028-04-09** | [Bex Andersen](people/bex-andersen) speaks on a panel at a B2B SaaS conference about what separates lasting infrastructure companies from feature-layer startups.
- **2028-06-11** | Crestline portfolio company in observability space begins informal design partner conversations with Anchor Codec."
}
